We've been discussing different code generation strategies but we haven't had the spare cycles to take a good stab at one. I did some experiments with numba a while ago and it looked promising because generating Python function objects is more convenient than writing a code printer, but then I noticed that numba doesn't generate blas/lapack calls and instead goes back into Python when you hit a dot. That kills my use case, which involves as lot of numerical linear algebra routines, so I haven't looked into it more (though I think numba knows how to handle cffi calls, so a [cffi-wrapped OpenBLAS-Lapack](https://bitbucket.org/mikefc/py-liblapack) might be all I need...).

One of the challenges is there are a lot of different directions to go. Maybe we should generate a Theano graph and let that compiler take over, or generate a TensorFlow graph and pass that off to the TF runtime. (A bit tangentially, we also want to wrap cupy.) We're just juggling too much to follow up on these things.

So we really appreciate your investigations here!


Here's some code to illustrate what I mean about numba:

```python
from numba import jit, float64
import numpy as np

square = jit(lambda x: x**2)
hypot = jit(lambda x, y: np.sqrt(square(x) + square(y)))
print hypot(3., 4.)
print hypot.inspect_asm()[(float64, float64)]

matrix_product = jit(lambda x, y: np.dot(x, y))
matrix_product(np.random.randn(3,3), np.random.randn(3))
print matrix_product.inspect_asm().values()[0]
```

The good bits from the first print statement after some unboxing:

```
	movsd	(%rsp), %xmm1
	mulsd	%xmm1, %xmm1
	movsd	8(%rsp), %xmm0
	mulsd	%xmm0, %xmm0
	addsd	%xmm1, %xmm0
	movabsq	$_numba.npymath.sqrt, %rax
	callq	*%rax
```

Yet the second one just generates a generic Python call (note the `_PyObject_CallFunctionObjArgs`).

I'm mostly thinking about the cases where bad equality checks have bit me, such as in dicts. If you have a class that happens to hash the same as another (modulo the dict size), and they compare as equal in Python, but they're not actually equal, you get some weird behavior. I spent hours tracking down why a Record was going into a dict but never coming out without raising an errors, and it was because my equality comparison was too loose.

Oh, I think it's a much simpler fix for you than you might think. If you change line 78 in records.py to say "type(self) == type(other)" then you'll get the metaclass's `__eq__()` invoked, which checks that the attribute names are the same. Then the rest of `RecordClass.__eq__()` checks for the values as you expect (by name).

That's something I certainly understand

> In my opinion, a lot of the proliferation of these command line arguments could be mitigated by giving users access to the date data before the date is constructed. Ideally, this would even allow you to generate an strptime string on the first run in pandas, which can then be applied to the remainder of the dates (and you can re-run the parser if you get a ValueError).

I am not sure if this would fix the issues for pandas (but besides that, something like that could certainly be useful).

To give a simplified overview of what pandas does in `to_datetime` for parsing strings to datetimes:

- if the user passed a `format` (like in `strptime`), then pandas uses its own (faster) parser
- if the user passed `infer_datetime_format=True` (default is now still False, but we want to change this), the format is inferred from the first value and if, using this inferred format, all values can be parsed without errors, this result is returned.
- try parsing the strings as ISO-like strings. If all values can be parsed without error, return this result
- If the above fails (this means: no format provided or inferred and not consistenly ISO-like format), then use the flexible `dateutil` parser

So we already have some functionality to infer the format and used that specification.

But we use `dateutil` if no format is defined, or if the format is not consistent. And it is actually a feature of pandas currently (relying on dateutil) that `to_datetime` *can* still handle messy data with inconsistent format eg ``['2012-01-01', '2012/01/02']``, which not be parsed by providing a `format`. 
So we actually want to use this flexibility, but at the same time be able to guarantee that the order of day/month *is* consistent / respects what is specified with `dayfirst`.

@david-richer-adsk I do not consider it a breaking change to drop support for a very old, past EOL minor version.

That said, we do use the `python-requires` metadata on PyPI which should indicate to `pip` that the last "safe" version of `dateutil` is 2.6.1. Of course, it's possible that `python-requires` was implemented in a version of `pip` that doesn't support Python 2.6, in which case you'll have to more actively manage your dependencies.

If you are worried that you may be deploying for both older and newer versions of Python, you can put version conditions in your `requirements.txt`, see [an example here](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/requirements-dev.txt#L2).  You'd want something like:

```
python-dateutil<=2.6.1; python_version == '2.6'
python-dateutil<3.0.0;  python_version != '2.6'
```
If `dateutil` is installed by one of your transitive dependencies, you'll probably just need to explicitly uninstall and reinstall version 2.6.1.

@BenGHolmes here are some steps to get started with the Redux architecture. If you have time, I'd recommend reading more about Redux [here](http://redux.js.org/).

- The global state of the application (called the "store") can only be mutated through an **action**. We already have an action that fetches the number of pending events, [defined here](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/actions/EventsActions.js#L24-L30). Calling that action will make an API request to the server and update the store with the results.
- We want to call the `countPending` action from inside [MainContainer](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/containers/MainContainer.js). The action should be called from the `componentDidMount` method, right after [this line](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/containers/MainContainer.js). *Note: you'll have to link `countPending` to `MainContainer`, similar to how the `setupToaster` is linked.*
- Now the store will be updated with the latest pending event count every time a page is loaded. In order to read the count from the store, we have to add a line to [this function](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/containers/MainContainer.js#L39-L44) that looks like this:

```js
events: state.app.events
```
This plucks the `events` portion of the store, which is simply an object containing all the application data related to events. To access this object inside `MainContainer`, you can now use `this.props.events`.
- Next, we need to pass some data to the `<Header />` component on [this line](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/containers/MainContainer.js#L31). I'll let you figure out how to do that, but here are some hints:
  - The pending event count is stored as an integer at `this.props.events.pending`. Its default value is `0`
  - If the pending count is `0`, the badge should not be rendered.

This looks like a pretty hard problem containing quantifiers and division. Internally all floating-point operations are translated into bit-vector operations, so in this case we end up with a QBF problem, which is often hard, but sometimes we can get lucky. On average, I would expect this type of problem not to be solved efficiently. 

The apparently "unrelated" first assertion contains the only quantifier. If that is removed, Z3 will use a completely different solver that doesn't support quantifiers (a pure SAT solver) and I would expect that, on average, to perform much better. 

Just to clarify: If there is a quantifier in the problem that the simplifier can't get rid of, Z3 will always chose the slow (SMT) solver instead of the SAT solver. It doesn't matter whether anything is "unrelated" or "easy", if a quantiifer is in the problem, Z3 will switch.

I'm fairly sure that there is no support for that in the interpolation modules and that there are no options for that yet either. It may be possible to construct some class of interpolant via normal, non-interpolation queries, but I'll let the experts answer those questions.

Hi Nikolaj,

Thanks a lot for the extra information, and sorry for my slow reply. I was indeed confusing multi-patterns with multiple ones - thanks for clarifying this!

Is it the case that the additional multi-patterns (above the set threshold) are guaranteed never to be used? Or are there situations in which the behaviour might change?

One reason that getting this threshold to the right level isn't entirely trivial for us is that we have code which generates patterns for quantifiers automatically. It's likely that cases with loads of different multi-patterns are rare, but we'd have to at least try to spot/count them to take this approach. Or I guess we could just set the parameter to something like 1000.

I like your edge example, but if I understand it correctly, this would be problematic even with just one multi-pattern. I guess in the end the number of multi-patterns doesn't determine whether one has the potential for bad triggering behaviour in a particular axiomatisation.

I totally agree that one probably doesn't want the patterns with this behaviour. But having them silently ignored is, I think, very hard for the user to understand. We are also working on tools which help with debugging problematic performance (due to triggering) in these cases, but we need the "full" behaviour of such examples in order to really analyse them.

More generally, I'd find any such situations much easier to debug if Z3 took all of the patterns and then gave us bad performance. I was lucky in this particular instance that a student managed to produce a test case which was quite easy to reduce, but in general it is really hard to detect that patterns written in the input file are not actually being used by the prover. It would be great to get a warning if this occurs in the default case.

It would also be nice to have an option to switch off this threshold. I would personally say that could be the default behaviour - a user writing "too many" multi-patterns will ultimately need to learn what the underlying problem in the encoding is, if they're going to get predictable results.

.. of course, I only realized a bit later that this is an arbitrary choice: the use of "for" forces the C++ API to be C++11 and not earlier. This was not the case before. I could have rewritten the three for loops to using iterators and avoided the issue. So in the end the right thing may be to not use C++11 features in the header file, even though applications would be able to use the new iterator that was introduced for convenience.

Here I propose a lightweight implementation of Bayes By Backprop[1] algorithm that is aimed on fitting posterior distribution.  That allows to make some decisions about out belief in prediction. It is possible to compute some metrics like mode, median, mean, variance and so on. For instance we can construct posterior confidence interval for prediction, compute the chance of mistake in some binary tasks. In most real world tasks it is crucial to now risks, i.e. medicine. 

I think that my implementation will cope with most Layers that exist in this repo and custom ones. They only need to add param with `add_param` method
## Usage example:
```python
# common imports
import lasagne
import theano.tensor as T
from lasagne.layers.dense import DenseLayer
from lasagne.layers.input import InputLayer
from lasagne.init import Normal
from lasagne.nonlinearities import tanh, sigmoid
#    Constants for more clear code
N_HIDDEN = 5
N_BATCHES = 100
```
### Before
```python
l_in = l_in = InputLayer((10,100))
l1_hidden = DenseLayer(l_in, num_units=N_HIDDEN, W=Normal(1), b=Normal(1),
                       nonlinearity=tanh)
l1_out = DenseLayer(l1_hidden, num_units=1, W=Normal(1), b=Normal(1),
                       nonlinearity=sigmoid)
# got it, now create objective
true_output = T.ivector('true_output')
net_output = lasagne.layers.get_output(l_output).ravel()
all_params = lasagne.layers.get_all_params(l_output)
objective = lasagne.objectives.binary_crossentropy(net_output, true_output)
objective = objective.sum()
# train it as you wish
```
### What I propose
```python
from lasagne.layers.bayes import (bbpwrap,
                                  NormalApproximation,
                                  NormalApproximationScMix,
                                  Accumulator)
#    1. Choose your favorite Layer
#    2. Wrap your favorite Layer
@bbpwrap(NormalApproximation(pstd=1))
class BayesDenseLayer(DenseLayer):
    pass
#    Gracias! It's bayesian!
#    And another
@bbpwrap(NormalApproximationScMix(pm1=.0, pstd1=.5, pm2=.0, pstd2=1e-3, pi=.5))
class BayesDenseLayer2(DenseLayer):
    pass
#    3. Create the thing that will do some dirty work for us,
#        it will collect all variational cost
acc = Accumulator()

l_in = InputLayer((10,100))

#    4. Pass `acc` as first argument
l1_hidden = BayesDenseLayer(acc, l_in, num_units=N_HIDDEN,
                             W=Normal(1), b=Normal(1),
                             nonlinearity=tanh)

#    Also possible to specify both mu and rho
myW = {'mu':Normal(1.5), 'rho':Normal(.1)}
l_output = BayesDenseLayer2(acc, l1_hidden, num_units=1,
                           W=myW, b=Normal(1),
                           nonlinearity=sigmoid)
net_output = lasagne.layers.get_output(l_output).ravel()
true_output = T.ivector('true_output')

#    5. That thing has collected the variational cost,
#       all we need is to get it and add to our objective
#       Do not forget to scale variational cost!
#       .. Note
#           Binary crossentropy is exactly the same as binomial
#           likelihood
objective = lasagne.objectives.binary_crossentropy(net_output,
                                                    true_output)
objective = objective.sum()
objective += (acc.get_cost() / N_BATCHES)

#    6. Choose adam optimizer for training
all_params = lasagne.layers.get_all_params(l_output)
updates = lasagne.updates.adam(objective, all_params)
```
Calculating them can be very useful. You can answer some real world questions like "what's tha chance that prediction is wrong?". Or you can calculate mean "confidence" for dataset and decide if it is overconfident or not.

Added a third commit that addresses what I mentioned as "next step" above.

This required some changes to the conv1d implementations in `lasagne.theano_extensions` (and accordingly more tests), so they're compatible with the new `conv2d` interface (specifically, support custom padding and filter flipping).
I also changed the tests to include test cases with custom padding for all conv2d tests (before this was only done for conv1d, and there was a single simplified padding test case per conv2d layer that just checked whether the output shape was correct -- probably because custom padding was unsupported in Conv2DLayer when the tests were written).
To reduce the number of test cases again, `untie_biases` is now a single test case rather than a parameter that's used with both settings for every test case.
Finally, now that all conv2d layers support`flip_filters`, I got rid of the `extra_kwargs` technique that modified all conv2d layers defaulting to `flip_filters=False` to use `flip_filters=True` in the tests. Instead, *all* conv2d test cases are now run with `flip_filters=True` except for a single extra test case trying `flip_filters=False`. This should get us covered without too much overhead.
We could probably drastically reduce the number of conv2d test cases, by the way, there are a lot of them right now:
```python
    for pad in (0, 1, 2, 'full', 'same'):
        for stride in (1, 2, 3):
            for filter_size in (1, 3):
```
We probably don't need a grid search like this and could just use a hand-picked selection of parameter combinations instead.

Well, I digged deeper into this and found out that VEX can handle the instruction `mrs`, but not the register used in that specific instruction `primask`. In summary, `mrs` as well as `msr` only supports `APSR` register (flag register), while in reality, these instructions might have other operands as `primask`, `control` or `basepri` (http://infocenter.arm.com/help/index.jsp?topic=/com.arm.doc.dui0489e/Cihibbbh.html).

The same goes for `svc` instruction (http://infocenter.arm.com/help/index.jsp?topic=/com.arm.doc.dui0489e/Cihibbbh.html): only the service number 0 (syscall) is handled by VEX.

I have sent a message in the VEX mailing list to ask about this, but haven't got the answer yet. Anw, I think it would be nice to have these instructions supported in angr.

This new patch series is way more than the one fix in question. Can you narrow it back to the fix?

Previously the HTTPClient first checked if a Charset was included in the Content-Type header, decoding the body with the given charset. After that it checked if the Content-Type is application/json, parsing the decoded body as json if nessesary. The Problem was if no Charset was included, the body never got decoded, which resulted in the above error.
I now changed it, so it first checks if the Content-Type is application/json and then decodes the body using a charset if provided. If no charset is provided it will decode with UTF-8 as charset.
I think this error occurred only recently because previously ddragon included a charset, which it looks like it doesn't anymore.

Hi @amitsaha, the "issue" is caused because `graphene` automatically maps `snake_case` field names and args to `camelCase` (as `camelCase` is the preferred way in js clients). So the good query will be
```python
schema.execute('{ helloId }`)
```

You can disable this auto camelCase behavior doing:
```python
schema = graphene.Schema(query=Query, auto_camelcase=False)
```

Regarding `graphene`, I think that the execution context should be added to `get_node`. As a good practice, the execution context should be available in all resolvers for this very reason.

In `graphql-django-view`, I use it to store the current `request` object from Django. But given your use-case, perhaps it would make more sense to pass it a `dict` containing your `session` and `auth` objects.

You should be able to do this using `graphene.core.Schema.execute()` by passing `request_context` as a kwarg to that function.

You are correct that it will still not be fully compliant with the spec but I would argue that it would be closer to the intended semantics.

I was not aware that a support library was required to make it fully spec-compliant. In my case I'm actually using graphene rather than graphql-core directly and it does not reformat its result in a way compliant with the spec either. IMHO I don't think it's unreasonable to expect the underlying implementation _should_ be spec compliant without any additional support libraries.

I will certainly move to using `sanic-graphql` regardless.

But the only issue is that there are some places where we need to signify that strict ordering is needed. For example, `collect_fields` will need to take OrderedDict instead of Dict, but that collected value is never returned as a result. Thus my concern with exposing the direct `map_type` to the API.

I've got some working code that overlaps this issue but don't have time this month to publish it! @abawchen, if you are diving into this, I have some insights you might consider:

For the case of embedded documents, an input registry is extremely useful (also might want to consider using input objects instead of direct kwargs). To automatically generate an InputObject, we're using:

```
def _create_input_factory(model, exclude_fields=('id', 'idempotency_key'), only_fields=()):
    return type(
        "Create{}Input".format(model.__name__),
        (MongoengineInputObjectType,),
        {"Meta": type('Meta', (), dict(model=model, exclude_fields=exclude_fields, only_fields=only_fields))}
    )

def generate_create_mutation(resolvable, only_fields=(), exclude_fields=()):
    model = get_model_from_resolvable(resolvable)
    assert hasattr(model, "idempotency_key")

    if not only_fields and not exclude_fields:
        only_fields = get_fields_from_resolvable(resolvable)
    CreateInput = _create_input_factory(model, only_fields=only_fields, exclude_fields=exclude_fields)

    CreateMutation = type(
        "Create{}".format(model.__name__),
        (graphene.Mutation,),
        {
            "mutate": partial(create_mutate, resolvable=resolvable, klass=lambda: CreateMutation),
            "success": graphene.Boolean(required=True),
            "{}".format(model.__name__.lower()): graphene.Field(resolvable),
            "Arguments": type(
                "Arguments",
                (),
                dict(
                    idempotency_key=graphene.ID(),
                    input=CreateInput(required=True)
                ))
        }
    )

    get_global_mutation_registry().register(CreateMutation, resolvable)
    return CreateMutation
```
We're creating a custom name for the mutation so the return type looks nicer in graphiql/docs, but creating a Payload object might be more broadly recognized. 

Update and delete are very similar. The api for using this in a schema is:
```
    create_mytype = generate_create_mutation(Mytype, only_fields=('myattr', 'myotherattr')).Field(description="Create a Mytype record.")
```

`MongoengineInputObjectType` is a fork/copypasta of `MongoengineObjectType` to use an `InputRegistry` instead. Also we created a version of `converter.py` which contains `convert_mongoengine_input_field` because `required=True` is different than `null=False`

```
from collections import OrderedDict
from graphene import InputField
from graphene.types.inputobjecttype import InputObjectType, InputObjectTypeOptions, InputObjectTypeContainer
from graphene.types.utils import yank_fields_from_attrs
from graphene_mongo.types import construct_self_referenced_fields
from graphene_mongo.utils import is_valid_mongoengine_model, get_model_fields
from mongoengine import ListField

def construct_fields(model, registry, only_fields, exclude_fields):
    _model_fields = get_model_fields(model)
    fields = OrderedDict()
    self_referenced = OrderedDict()
    for name, field in _model_fields.items():
        is_not_in_only = only_fields and name not in only_fields
        is_excluded = name in exclude_fields
        if is_not_in_only or is_excluded:
            # We skip this field if we specify only_fields and is not
            # in there. Or when we exclude this field in exclude_fields
            continue
        if isinstance(field, ListField):
            # Take care of list of self-reference.
            document_type_obj = field.field.__dict__.get('document_type_obj', None)
            if document_type_obj == model._class_name \
                or isinstance(document_type_obj, model) \
                or document_type_obj == model:
                self_referenced[name] = field
                continue
        converted = convert_mongoengine_input_field(field, registry)
        if not converted:
            continue
        fields[name] = converted

    return fields, self_referenced


class MongoengineInputObjectTypeOptions(InputObjectTypeOptions):
    model = None
    registry = None  # type: InputRegistry


class MongoengineInputObjectTypeContainer(InputObjectTypeContainer):

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        dict.__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)
        for key in self._meta.fields.keys():
            setattr(self, key, self.get(key, None))


class MongoengineInputObjectType(InputObjectType):

    @classmethod
    def __init_subclass_with_meta__(cls, model=None, input_registry=None, skip_input_registry=False,
                                    only_fields=(), exclude_fields=(), **options):

        assert is_valid_mongoengine_model(model), (
            'You need to pass a valid Mongoengine Model in {}.Meta, received "{}".'
        ).format(cls.__name__, model)

        if not input_registry:
            input_registry = get_global_input_registry()

        assert isinstance(input_registry, InputRegistry), (
            'The attribute input_registry in {} needs to be an instance of '
            'InputRegistry, received "{}".'
        ).format(cls.__name__, input_registry)

        converted_fields, self_referenced = construct_fields(
            model, input_registry, only_fields, exclude_fields
        )
        mongoengine_fields = yank_fields_from_attrs(converted_fields, _as=InputField)

        _meta = MongoengineInputObjectTypeOptions(cls)
        _meta.model = model
        _meta.registry = input_registry
        _meta.fields = mongoengine_fields

        if not _meta:
            _meta = InputObjectTypeOptions(cls)

        fields = OrderedDict()
        for base in reversed(cls.__mro__):
            fields.update(
                yank_fields_from_attrs(base.__dict__, _as=InputField)
            )

        if _meta.fields:
            _meta.fields.update(fields)
        else:
            _meta.fields = fields

        container = type(cls.__name__, (MongoengineInputObjectTypeContainer, cls), mongoengine_fields)
        _meta.container = container

        super(InputObjectType, cls).__init_subclass_with_meta__(
            _meta=_meta, **options)

        if not skip_input_registry:
            input_registry.register(cls)
            # Notes: Take care list of self-reference fields.
            converted_fields = construct_self_referenced_fields(self_referenced, input_registry)
            if converted_fields:
                mongoengine_fields = yank_fields_from_attrs(converted_fields, _as=InputField)
                cls._meta.fields.update(mongoengine_fields)
                input_registry.register(cls)
```

You can use `exclude_fields` in `SQLAlchemyObjectType`. Lets say you want to hide `private_field` and `another_private_field` on `Example` (your SQLAlchemy model).
```python
from graphene_sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemyObjectType
from .models import Example as ExampleModel

class Example(SQLAlchemyObjectType):
    class Meta:
        model = ExampleModel
        exclude_fields = ('private_field', 'another_private_field', )
```

@bjwatson After discussing with @lukesneeringer, I'm opposed to this for a few reasons, mostly related to proto dependencies:

- It requires `artman` AND `toolkit` to know not only about the GAPIC config for the API it is generating, but about *all* GAPIC configs in existence (since protos can import arbitrary other protos)
- A change the configured GAPIC package name in one API potentially entails breaking changes across any API that depends on its protos
- We don't have any good way to handle protos for APIs where there is no GAPIC wrapper, since there is no GAPIC config. Furthermore, introducing a GAPIC config down the road for those protos is a breaking change to any API that relies on them

> Let's not merge this yet. We should also update toolkit.

> KeyError: 'HAVE_DOUBLE_THREADS'

so I'm not able to add the test to the repo and handle your request via a pull request.

Below is a short ``pytest`` test that properly exercises the issue. I would expect it to be quite easy to convert to the ``unittest`` framework that you use, but let me know if you need help (or if you can suggest what the above error means, I can try to do it myself).

``` 
from __future__ import division
from builtins import object

import pytest
import hashlib

import numpy as np
import pyfftw


def rfftn(a, s=None, axes=None):
    return pyfftw.interfaces.numpy_fft.rfftn(a, s=s, axes=axes,
                overwrite_input=False, planner_effort='FFTW_MEASURE')


def hash(x):
    return hashlib.md5(x).hexdigest()


class TestSet01(object):

    def setup_method(self, method):
        N = 64
        pyfftw.interfaces.cache.enable()
        np.random.seed(12345)
        self.U = np.random.randn(N, N).astype(np.float32)
        self.V = np.random.randn(N, N).astype(np.float32)


    def test_01(self):
        # Test for pyfftw bug (on certain architectures) that corrupts the
        # content of argument variables. In this test, the bug copies the
        # content of V into U or vice-versa.
        U = self.U
        V = self.V
        uh = hash(U)
        vh = hash(V)
        x = rfftn(U, None, axes=(0,1))
        y = rfftn(V, None, axes=(0,1))
        assert(uh == hash(U))
        assert(vh == hash(V))
        x = rfftn(U, None, axes=(0,1))
        y = rfftn(V, None, axes=(0,1))
        assert(uh == hash(U))
        assert(vh == hash(V))
```

`std::shared_ptr` could be added in a backwards compatible way. It would require SFINAE-ing the holder declaration macro:
```c++
#define PYBIND11_DECLARE_HOLDER_TYPE(type, holder_type) \
    namespace pybind11 { namespace detail { \
    template <typename type> \
    class type_caster<holder_type, enable_if_t<!is_shared_ptr<holder_type>::value>> \
        : public type_caster_holder<type, holder_type> { }; \
    }}
```
It would turn off any existing user-declared `std::shared_ptr` holders (and leave the non-SFINAEd library specialization). The only downside is that it doesn't have a deprecation warning so it would essentially leave a bit of dead code for existing users.

I have a couple of small changes I'd personally like to see, but otherwise, something like that looks good! I like the approach you came up with of using `sys.stdout` or an argument passed into the function better than what I suggested of having the function return both the patch and stdout.

The small changes I'd like to see are:

1. We merged the same code in another PR, but instead of using `_, _ = args, kwargs` to stop warnings from our linter, let's either rename `args` and `kwargs` to `unused_args` and `unused_kwargs`, or add `# pylint: disable=unused-argument`.
2. We only write the message to stdout for the notification method. Rather than possibly adding additional parameters in the future to change this behavior (or just suddenly making the change), I think we should do it for all `IDisplay` methods. The way I'd do this is make another local function that takes `*args` and  `**kwargs`, passes them to `_assert_valid_call`, and then passes them to `write_msg`.
3. I think the approach of setting `write_msg` as the `notification` method directly prevents people from using normal `mock` functionality on that method such as making assertions about call count, parameters, etc. Instead, I'd use the `func` parameter to the `FreezableMock` like we do for other methods.
4. I personally don't like the asymmetry between what `_create_get_utility_mock` and `_create_get_utility_mock_with_stdout` return. One returns a `FreezableMock` object and one returns a function that returns a `FreezableMock` object. Instead of this, I'd make `patch_get_utility_with_stdout` call `_create_get_utility_mock_with_stdout` and pass the return value as the `new` argument to [mock.patch](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html#unittest.mock.patch).

> Just to make this point clear, Raiden can and does retry sending messages, the messages are enqueued in-order and they have a monotonically increasing transferred amount for this reason, iow the messages are idempotent and can be sent multiple times.

Ofcourse, but the whole point is that if the user can't have an idempotent transfer interface then "raiden transfers" are not idempotent.  At this point with the release so close we do care about what the user can and can not do and we do define raiden by it.

## Solution

> The user cannot call the transfer method twice because the interface exposed to the user is not idempotent, if an idempotent interface should be expose we could require the user to send the transferred_amount instead of the the transfer amount, another option is to use the identifiers to check if the transfer is already in flight.

> Maybe we could enforce the identifier uniqueness (and have it signed too, somehow) to evaluate (on both sides) the value increment/signing?
> I agree with this approach.

We need to provide the user with the ability to create idempotent direct transfers. We can't stay with the current model. So it seems we have two choices then.

### Use the identifier

So far we were regarding the identifer only as something to be used by applications built on top of raiden and not as a part of our protocol. The original [issue](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/139) left its purpose of existence a bit vague.

How would it work?

We could have a map of identifiers to direct transfers in flight and if another user request comes for a direct transfer that is in flight and in the map we simply ignore it.

Anything more?

### Expose the transferred amount

Agreeing on the transferred amount and exposing that interface to the user to have the ability to do idempotent transfers is also possible. The problem with this approach is that it does not sound very intuitive to the user. How would it work?

I have already sent 4 tokens to Bob. I now want to send another 2 tokens to Bob so I am going to sign a message with 6 being the transferred amount. The problem is a user interface problem. How to show to the user that these 2 tokens he is signing to send are gonna bring his total balance to `deposit - 6` and not to `deposit - 8` or `deposit - 10` if for some reason he tries to repeat the transfer.

At the moment I am inclined to say we should go for the identifier approach.

I think the key difference between sos and other mechanisms such as JSON is the "neutrality". Where as data exchange using a common file format (e.g. JSON or extension of JSON) is much easier to implement, it can not be optimized for large datasets (e.g. huge dataframe) and can not accommodate language-specific datatypes (e.g. series vs named tuples). SoS sacrifices neutrality and requires the specification of "from" and "to" languages, but allows a de-centralized implementation of "type A of language B" to "type C of language D".  However, in practice, there are certainly many cases where users knows the from and to languages and would be willing to specify such information in exchange for lossless (meaning less, not no) data exchange. Implementation-wise, as soon as we define a common interface, the implementation can be separated into numerous small and independent functions that could be implemented gradually.

So I do think this is valid project that can be of interest to a large audience.

When the first actor goes out of scope, it should release the GPU resource. For example, doing

```
actor1 = GPU.remote()
del actor1
```

should release the GPU resources. Normally you shouldn't have to call `del`, it should just happen when `actor1` goes out of scope.

Note that the GPU resources are not released synchronously. That is, a "destructor" task actually is scheduled on the actor and runs and releases the resources, so if you do 

```
actor1 = GPU.remote()
del actor1
actor2 = GPU.remote()
```

it probably won't work, but it will work if you do

```
import time

actor1 = GPU.remote()
del actor1
time.sleep(2)
actor2 = GPU.remote()
```

Performance tips:
 - more cores generally means more performance for the s3 and s3-file driver
   - I test with a c4.2xlarge, even more performance if you go bigger. e.g. c4.4xlarge, c4 8xlarge.
   - I found t1/t2 instance performance to s3 to be on the low side.
 - Go big on tile size
 - Go bigger on chunking parameter to arrive at 10-20MB in size.
 - After ingest to S3, it takes around 1-2 hours for s3 performance to stabilise.

I noticed the way you ingest to EBS eith the s3-file driver is incorrect which is probably why you are still seeing netcdf files.

The driver should be s3-test.

E.g.

container: '/home/ubuntu/data/output'
storage:
  driver: s3-test

The container parameter must be set for s3 or s3-file ingest.

针对以上的使用代码，分析如下：

但是无论如何，使用调用更为底层（引擎层）的 API ，均可解决上述的问题，更方面的使用底层API是本 PR 的主要要解决的问题。

In theory, yes, although I think you'd really want to pre-train this thing with historic data if you can. If your timesteps are say minutes, or even seconds, training live could take a pretty long time.  A lot of the benchmark programs that just do things like learn to play atari and whatnot can take hundreds of thousands or even millions of timesteps to reach their optimal policy.

Also, might want a different policy for running live vs training. Like you might want to just run a pure greedy policy live, but then have your training do something else, like epsilon-greedy or whatever so it doesn't get stuck learning a sub-optimal policy.

But yeah in general if you have a trained system, you'd just save the weights, then load them again when you're ready to use it live.

This is the updated version of the Earley work previously in #77.

This is significantly cleaner now and has a bunch more performance optimisations in it. It should be fast and use a very small memory footprint.

A couple of notes:
1) As per the discussion on #77, this is a breaking change for ambiguity='explicit'. ambiguity='explicit' will now return the root node of the SPPF. Implication is, if you want to work with ambiguities you need to work with the SPPF. Although a slightly higher learning curve this in the end is significantly better for folks using explicit ambiguity; as the original explicit ambiguity trees had quite a few problems in practical usage.
2) There are some tests still failing, all (I think) are due to the new ambiguity resolvers; as I couldn't work out how to fully replicate all the behaviours of the original ambiguity resolvers and get the exact same determinism. I opted to leave the tests failing so others could take a look.  I'd appreciate if someone (@erezsh ?) can take a look and perhaps give some feedback or tweak the new ambiguity resolvers (see earley_forest.py) to be more deterministic or better replicate the originals.
3) This is pretty damn quick now. There are some more performance improvements I have waiting in the wings, but I want to hold out on those until this change gets merged and/or battle tested as they will make the code slightly less readable; but are worth it for significant performance improvements (30-40% minimum improvement + additional memory savings).

I did just check, and the path reported by `zmq.get_includes()` for zmq.utils was wrong, so that too will be fixed when PR #172 is merged.  I will think about splitting off allocate.h and some of the generic utilities (memcpy, ec.) from libzmq.pxd, but I cannot do that with zmq_compat.h, which is required for libzmq.pxd to work with zmq.h from both libzmq-2.1 and 3.1.
